video_title: Fireside Chat with Sal Khan and Dr. Amy Alzina

segments.text: Hello everyone. Uh thank you so much for
being here. Um I'm joined today by two
people who I have a lot of respect for
and I think um we're all going to get a
lot out of this conversation. For those
of you who have not met Dr. Amy Alzena,
she is the definition of a trailblazer.
She does a lot of things somehow also
runs. Uh she's the superintendent of
Cold Spring School District, one of the
top academically performing districts in
the state. She's uh she chairs the
ACSA's artificial intelligent task force
which um one of my colleagues sits on as
well and uh so I'm very aware of the
amazing work. Um and then she's super
I'm sure I'm missing some things cuz
there are a lot but Amy has been a real
friend to other districts throughout
California um supporting them in this
like very exciting kind of turbulent
moment as AI has hit education. S, I
don't think you need an introduction,
but I will just say that the the work
that we're doing right now uh is is
something I think everyone in this room
is watching closely. So, thank you for
being here, both of you.
>> Thank you.
>> Yeah, last week was a big one. Um you
were both together at Q. Um S, Amy, you
sat on a panel with Dr. Ruth Perez from
LA County. Um
there's a lot of honest conversation uh
with people who are really running
districts. So Amy, you were in both the
panel and then in a smaller round table
group with S. Has anything stuck out to
you that you can share with us?
>> Oh wow. Well, what resonated with me in
that our discussion afterwards was when
we were talking about in California the
new math framework that came out and how
everyone's just jumping at adopting a
curriculum and I I even told myself,
wait, am I missing something here or is
this do we change the standards? What's
happening? and and S you were saying
something you know yeah it takes a
couple years to understand a curriculum
and I had just walked into um one of my
teachers classrooms whose math charts
math student achievement is off the
charts and it was a third grade teacher
yesterday and I was just doing a deeper
dive on what is it that she's doing with
her students and she said really it was
last year when I had a new partner
teacher I just hired her from my
neighboring district and we did a deeper
dive on the curriculum and we really
identified some holes and how we needed
to restructure the curriculum. Well,
here we are fast forward with the same
curriculum seven years later, right? And
still learning that curriculum so we can
get better at it. What works, what
doesn't work, how we're utilizing Khan
Academy as a supplement. And um I can
only think in my head of like the what's
going to happen right now as districts
are just adopting these new crazy
curriculum and what's going to happen to
math achievement [laughter]
and but s what you said really resonated
with me how it it just takes a couple
years to understand a new math
curriculum and you're absolutely right.
So I don't I you know that was my long
winded way of saying wow um what's
happening in the state right now.
>> Yeah super appreciate that s did you
hear that too curious if you saw it
differently heard it differently than
Amy did. No, definitely got that. And
actually, it makes me think about, you
know, we all spoke a lot and and I
always try to speak a lot about uh on
technology. You shouldn't just use
technology for the sake of it. You
should first take a step back and say
like what are my goals here? Like what
am I trying to what am I trying to solve
for? And it I think in the context we're
talking about it's usually we want to
improve academic outcomes oftentimes is
measured by state assessments, although
there's a whole other thread about that
too. Um, and the a and the the theory of
action that we've had and I think Amy
and others have had is hey if we can
allow students to really fill in their
gaps, learn at their own pace,
personalize, get a lot of deliberate
practice that that's going to improve
outcomes and that's exactly what we try
to do. So what you know whether it's
using videos or software or AI or
whatever we're trying to do on the
technology side those are that that's
truly the goal. If there's a way to do
it with pencil and paper let's do it
with pencil and paper. We don't need to
use the fanciest technology. I think
this uh curricular discussion that Amy's
bringing up is actually the same thing.
And you know, on the actual curricular
decisions, I'm always a little bit of a
of a bystander, but I've I we've Aviv,
you know, we've paid close attention to
it at Khan Academy for the last 15 or 20
years because we're always aligning to
different standards, aligning to
different curricula. And so I almost
have a unique vantage point where I'm,
you know, I'm doing the the the common
core and Florida best and I'm still
making videos for it. Just yesterday I
made some, you know, illustrative math
videos. I made some Florida best videos.
So I see and and the reality is the
heart of all of these curricula are the
good parts are the same and and what you
really want is um students to get more
practice at their zone of proximal
development. You want a teacher who
feels comfortable um driving that
holding the students accountable digging
in deeper when appropriate using tools
like Khan Academy where appropriate for
personalization. And every time people
are upset about test scores or they
don't think things the reaction seems to
be and it seems to be about every seven
or eight years um let's get new
curricula and there's you know the Gates
Foundation ran a big study last year on
high quality instructional materials and
this isn't to you know throw shade on it
but but they couldn't see an an effect
um there um and and as Amy just
mentioned every time you do that change
management you kind of lose two three
years like just just when you were um
getting good at the old curric I I would
argue that someone who is uh a teacher
who's really strong at at a curriculum
from 1980, especially in math, I mean,
we could talk about other things, but
that's going to be a good curriculum.
And and someone who's struggling with
the modern curricula, it's it's going to
be harder. Another thing we did talk
about, which I find fascinating, it's
obviously a a bigger notion, is so much
of um state so so much of of the
perceived quality of schools, etc. are
based on standardized tests. Um there's
some merit to that, but maybe there's an
opportunity to broaden the aperture. Uh
right now, uh you know, there's things
that we know are going to matter more
and more in an AI world, like
communication, collaboration,
entrepreneurship. I mean, we could go
down the list and those those types of
things aren't captured uh right now, but
maybe there are ways to capture it. So,
that was the other interesting part of
the conversation we had with some
district leaders.
>> Yeah. I'm curious. Actually, I'm
remembering now that I wanted to ask you
about this. Um Ruth Perez was was with
you last week, right?
>> Yes.
>> Uh assistant superintendent of of LA
County, which on the surface at least
seems like it could not be more
different than Cold Spring. Were you
thinking about that at all, Amy? Like
what do you know each other? Were you do
do you know anything about how that
district works? And
>> yeah, great question. You know, I get
that a lot because we're a single school
district. We're small. I came from Santa
Barbara Unified where I was 18 years,
principal of two different schools and
low soioeconomic, different demographics
and um what I have learned through my 20
years as administrator is exactly what
Sal said is when students are working
within within their zone of proximal
development, students are engaged and
student achievement increases. And um I
found that in my doctoral work why I had
the best teachers ever and they were
super [sighs] loving and kind but
students were reading at a too high of a
level or too low of a level so they
weren't making growth and I was
wondering what's going on. So I had to
do a deeper dive. So it's for all areas
right? We all have to be working in our
sweet spot and it doesn't matter if
you're in a large school district or a
small school district. That's how we all
learn. And so, um, going back to your
question, um, no, you know, I I because
we're talking about kids and all kids,
um, learn the same and it's identifying
what gets them excited in the morning,
what is their purpose and how do we
connect with them um, so that they want
to come to school in the morning and not
just the core subject areas, but our
integrative approach to teaching and
learning. And that's really the
heartbeat of what we do um in education.
>> Um
I love that. Um and I I wish we had more
time cuz I want to ask you about the
transition from principal to
superintendent. So if we have time,
we'll come back to that. But um Sal, I
wonder when you see success like this,
right? Like academic achievement like we
see in Cold Spring, what kind of
questions arise for you uh around
scalability?
Yeah, I think that's that's the ultimate
question. Um because we know
that if um and we see this in multiple
efficacy studies at Khan Academy, you
know, if if students are engaged on the
platform, they are seeing pretty
dramatic growth. And people don't have
to believe me, you know, go there's a
lot of there's 50 plus, most of those
are thirdparty studies um that we did
not, you know, in any way fund or
anything. Um and they they all show that
if students are able to get even let's
call it 30 to 60 minutes a week which
isn't a lot um they're growing depending
on the study 20 to 60% more than more
than uh their their peers. And you know
we have a few special cases of of places
where they're more like 30 to 60 minutes
a day and those type of places we're
seeing growth of in some cases two to
three grade levels a year. So, um, we
know if if if the students are engaged
there, it it's a and and and there's no
magic there. It really is working on
your zone of proximal development,
getting a lot of and getting immediate
feedback. Now, we know that from there
are there's maybe five or 10% of
students that if you just throw Khan
Academy at them and say go, they get
excited and they'll just be off to the
races. But we know that most students
aren't that. they need a really quality
classroom environment um in in order to
engage. And what we have learned
there there's always been really from
the beginning of Khan Academy uh tens if
not hundreds of thousands of teachers
who are also kind of this let's call it
lean into Khan Academy saw a lot of that
growth but it was in it was in pockets
and so when we decided to do the
district offering about eight years ago
and we wanted to do more systemically
with districts the big question is is um
will a district especially if we're
talking about large districts be able to
get that type of consistency across of
it and and and I I won't pretend like
it's a completely solved problem. I
think you have leaders like Amy where um
she's, you know, she this is one of her
top priorities. She believes in it.
She's a force of nature. You know,
everyone when Amy leaves the room, every
like she's a force of nature. She is in
the best in the best possible way. Um
and and I think when you have investment
at at the top like that, it does start
to really pay those dividends. And we've
seen that with Amy. We've seen that in
large urban school districts uh like
Newark, New Jersey. But it's when the
the the superintendent, the chief
academic officer, someone says, "Okay,
you know, this is going to be my top
priorities. I'm going to monitor the
data. I'm going to provide soft
accountability. Let's reward the the
teachers who are engaged a lot and let's
figure out how we can support the
teachers um who need more help."
>> Would you agree, Amy?
>> I love that 100%. Um, and we see the
same results. You know, whether again
going back to doesn't matter what school
you're at, the teacher that implements
with Fidelity and really connects with
kids, you're going to see a 2x growth. I
mean, I I literally just met with a
teacher, again, another teacher
yesterday. Um, the average growth that
we expect is between 40 to 60 um,
student growth index. Her class average
is 92. And this is our largest class of
fourth grade students. We know the
fourth grade students are challen it's a
challenging sta group of standards but
she does what's called an infield
outfield model where she's working with
a core group of students the other
students are working independently at
their level and they are so
intrinsically motivated because also
with it comes goal setting right it it's
not just hey Khan Academy and go no
let's set personal goals what are your
goals are what is it that the actions
that you're going to take to achieve
those goals so you have that sense of
purpose and then Lex support you so you
can meet those goals and that's why
those students outperform I mean every
single year 100% of those students not
only meet the standards but they score
advance and that includes students with
severe learning disabilities right
because they're all working at their
level um and so I I I couldn't agree
more but it's not just hey just turn on
the computer and I don't know why my
kids aren't growing Well, because
there's no interaction with the student,
right? There's no relationship with the
student. And I love what you S has in
his book. I'm going to bring out this
old one of yours, S.
You have a chapter in here. Um it's it's
called uh teaching as a team sport,
right? And you talk in this chapter
about coaching in here as teachers as
coaches and and not as sage on the
stage, right? And how important it is to
be that coach and to have opportunities
for student choice. And what I loved as
I was interviewing my students and how
they utilize Khan Academy, they love the
choice. I'm going to work on unit two or
I'm going to work on unit three. I love
that there's choice and we love that as
adults. Nobody likes to be told what to
do, right? And they loved to progress
monitor. Oh, here it is. I met this goal
and now I'm going to move on to the next
one. I mean, literally there was like
almost like you could tell this
intrinsic competition. I want to get to
fifth grade because I know this, right?
And that's excitement about learning and
and that's not a model that's new. I
mean, my education, we all take our, you
know, what we learn from our own
education. I literally had a model. It
was called Accelerated Christian
Education. It was It was ACE. It was all
work pages. It was goal setting. You do
five work pages a day. You had a goal
chart, but you could get extra credit
points and they were worth five cents
for each page you did. And so I
literally skipped two grades in math
because I wanted to like I I want
intrinsically I was like, I'm going to
buy those roller skates for my sister
out of the J C Penney catalog. I still
remember I bought her Smurf roller
skates. But it's that same type of
concept of here's the goal and you're
going to get rewarded if you go past
that goal, right? And if I have a
question I like it literally, this was
this is going to be sound funny, but you
put up your flag and and then the
teacher went around and answered your
question, right? But that worked well
for me, but I also saw my friend where I
like needed a lot more support from the
teacher. So the teacher was with like my
friend more, right? So
it's very similar with technology. Now
it makes it easier. Now I don't have a
workbook. Now I can just do it adaptive
learning. [laughter]
>> That's too much, but yes.
>> No, you're great. Um S, I wonder if it
makes sense for you to speak a little
bit now to we kind of rebuilt Khan
Academy. Amy just shared with me before
you hopped on that they're actually
rolling out the reimagined experience
this weekend which is exciting, but can
you um talk to us a little bit about why
is rebuilt with some of the things Amy
shared?
>> Yes. And Amy, feel free to send
immediate feedback. I you know the
freshest feedback as [laughter] you roll
out. There's a lot of uh changes we
made. We're just for everyone to know
we're in kind of a a pilot mode right
now. So we've been making a lot of
changes. So hopefully the the sum of
those changes Amy will be very
impressed. But the the the catalyst for
it um uh there there's many things that
we've we we've known that we need to do
better um on our classroom experience.
One of I think one thing we've done very
well as Amy mentioned is give giving
students a lot of choice, a lot of
agency on the platform. But in let's
call it the classic Khan Academy, the
one that's out there today. uh we have
gotten the clear feedback that that is
great but some students or maybe all
could also benefit from some direction
too like when in doubt what's the next
thing that I should work on or the next
two or three things so one thing that's
in Khan Academy reimagined is it's a
very clear learning path that's almost
the most obvious visually thing you know
the students will see their learning
path now they can also continue to have
the view that they've that they're maybe
used to if they have been using Khan
Academy where they can see all the units
etc etc ETA. Um, another catalyst,
frankly, was u
federal accessibility guidelines. We
were saying, "Okay, what would it take
for us to redo all of our current?"
We're like, "No, let's just rebuild it
all and do it uh so it's all it's all
accessible." We knew that a lot of the
teacher tools, uh, you know, maybe I
shouldn't share what what we sometimes
call our own, uh, experience in house,
but we've we sometimes called it a
Winchester Mystery House. For any of
y'all who have been to San Jose, there's
this old mansion that I think the the
the woman who lived there 100 years ago
was trying to confuse a ghost and um
built a lot of stair staircases to
nowhere or doors that just drop into a
pit and things like that. I I don't
think we were that bad. But um I think
any product that when you have years and
years and years of incremental change
and someone asks you, hey, add this, add
that, we're like, oh yes, and you add
it, but it doesn't it's not maybe not as
easy to find. You have to hunt and peck
and and click around a lot. So we really
wanted to make it much clearer on how to
use it, how to onboard it. Another
thing, you know, there's so many use
cases in education. Uh there's the
teacher who might want to make the
one-off assignment. There's the the
teacher who want wants to do the more
personalized learning. Uh students
learning at their own time and pace. And
historically, we part of the Winchester
Mystery House is we were trying to meet
all of these and we still are. But I
think in this new product, you're going
to see a stronger opinion um that we
really do think the personalized mastery
learning. Teachers can still make
one-off assignments, but this one
definitely has a bias towards teachers
assigning the units for the year being
very easy to monitor it. And we're
already seeing really good data in the
pilots that when teachers assign the
units for the year, we're seeing
depending on on the classroom, we're
looking at 50 60% increase in skills
leveled up. um on on Khan Academy. So
loving that part. Um Kigo the AI I think
the first version it was very much this
thing that sits on the side and it had
some benefits but once again when it
just sits on the side it's like a tutor
that says hey come ask me a question and
a lot of students might not have know
how to ask the question or get the
question. We're now it's it's it's a
much more central character and and once
again not just for the sake of throwing
AI in there. it is to drive engagement
to make sure kids are reflecting when
they get something wrong, maybe get
extra opportunities.
Similarly, on the teacher side, it acts
much more as a proactive teaching
assistant. Uh, you know, internally
we've called it, you know, KIGO
concierge, Khan Academy concierge.
Anything that the teacher needs to do,
they can do it conversationally now. Uh,
but those are those are kind of the um
oh, and there's there's more game
mechanics. So, there's a fun live
classroom game. We actually only
launched that about month or two ago and
that one is dramatically increasing
engagement. So that the first 30, you
know, when the students work for 30
minutes on Khan Academy, we're seeing
almost a 4x in terms of how how many
skills the k kids are leveling up in
those 30 minutes. And that's because
their posture changes because it's a
game. And I've seen that with my own
11-year-old. I I always used to ask him,
"Oh, how much progress you get on Khan
Academy?" And you know, I on a good day
he used to tell me 1% 2%. Now, now he'll
say like 8%. I'm like, "What happened?
How did you get 8% of a whole course
[laughter] in a day?" And he's like, "Oh
no, teacher Mike, he he did the
classroom game and I love [laughter] him
and his buddies." Uh, and and then
there's some fun stuff like
leaderboards, too, that we think we we
see early signals that it's it's having
a fun cooperative co-op coopetition
spirit. And that's a best practice that
we saw out of a lot of school districts.
They would do it on poster board and
send out emails. And once again, it's
fun coopetition, light accountability.
Uh but we think these types of things
will drive engagement.
>> Exciting.
>> Kids are going to love that.
>> Yeah.
>> The gamified component.
>> Yes.
>> So, we know we know gamified components
of learning are effective. We've learned
a lot about how to move students forward
with technology. I can't help but bring
this conversation to a a point about
screen time because it's every headline,
right? Um Amy, you are a parent and a
principal and a superintendent. Maybe
you can start us off like what are you
hearing? What are you feeling? What do
you have to report on?
>> Yeah, I think every parent's worried
about screen time, right? And and um
including me, you know, my kids are 18
and 20, well adults now, right?
[laughter] That you're worried about
screen time. And you know, and that's
why I tell everyone in Salvador me say
this all the time is you have to start
with the common sense media digital
citizenship lessons. Like this is where
we did it wrong with social media. You
know, secondary assumed, elementary did
it, elementary assumed, secondary taught
the lesson. So the students never got
them. Um, but what they also do is they
not only teach the student, they teach
the teacher because you're learning, oh
wow, I never saw the bias in AI or I
didn't understand this. So I think
that's important in and students
understanding that screen time can be
addictive and and so for us at
ColdSpring I get this question a lot
especially because I'm you know AI task
force chair I think parents so worried
that our kids are on devices all the
time which is not the case right and at
all I mean we are so whole child um I
said you know they're probably on a
device
max maybe in fourth through sixth grade
an hour a day I would say um in the the
lower grades more like 20 to 40 a
minutes and I and I say our kids aren't
getting addicted tech to technology at
school I'm just going to be honest I
tell you parents it's at home I mean
we're utilizing it as a babysitter and I
said I you know what I'm at fault right
here's a phone you're at dinner you're
out to dinner you need your little one
to be quiet here watching watch this
movie, right? You're on a plane, watch
this. You know, you're on a car ride.
And so, they're getting more technology
outside of school than they are within
the school day. I mean, that's just the
honest truth. Um and so yeah, it is an
issue, but I think um it is frustrating.
I know as educators when we hear this,
you know, um argument brought up a lot
that we as educators are causing our
kids to be addicted to technology when
um in fact that's not the case. It it is
frustrating.
>> S what are what are you hearing? I'll
let you respond. Yeah, I always like
once again take a step back and no don't
make it like about technology versus not
technology because I think even before
the first computer entered a classroom
you could visit two different classrooms
and say oh this is one where this is
really good for the kids and then
another one where you're like hey I
think the kids are kind of brain rotting
even before technology existed and the
difference the good one the one that you
want is a lot of what we already talked
about there's active learning the kids
are engaged the teacher is engaged. Um,
they're helping each other. Uh, they're
they're getting feedback on the things
that they need to get feedback on. And
an and a not so great classroom is the
kids are, you know, kind of just trying
to sit in the chair, kind of passive,
fingers on their lips. I mean, you know,
some of us grew up in classrooms, not
all of them. There were some great
classrooms where where I grew up, too,
but there some were like this. You're
just staring at the clock. Most of the
class is either lost or bored. Um,
you're not allowed to talk to each
other. That's how one of the best ways
that human beings actually learn,
obviously it has to be controlled in in
a reasonable way. But that's not um that
that's not ideal either. And so I think
if we go into this world where now
technology is another variable and 100%
agree with everyone, it is it can be
very addictive. So you have to be very
very careful with it. But I think what
we need to optimize for is but if we're
talking about the classroom environments
that are active learning either at your
zone of proximal development and or
learning other what do you would call it
soft skills durable skills of
communication intrapersonal skills uh
it's being more personalized etc. So,
it's not I if if a student is um using a
screen to edit some really cool video
they're making, if they're using a
screen for digital art, if they're using
a screen to write a paper, um if and and
actually use it, do it themselves, if
they're using a screen for research, if
they're using a screen for Khan Academy,
um I think those are are solid uses. And
you know on average yeah you don't want
it too much but I think any of us who've
gone deep on something sometimes you get
in focus mode and you might spend a
couple of hour you know when I when I
got really into programming when I was
in high school yeah there were days
where I would just get super focused and
12 hours I'm like where did that time
go? But I wrote a cool application. That
is not bad as long as I still had
friends. I'm still going out. I'm still
getting exercise. I'm still interacting
etc. etc. So, I think that's what we
want to optimize for. Um, and yeah, we
want to look, cell phones have no
business definitely in in in the school
setting and at home. I mean, we're it's
not just kids, all of us. I I I ruined
30 minutes of my morning this, you know,
just reading the news. I'm like, what
happened to my [laughter] time? So, and
if we and if our frontal loes are
apparently more developed, they're going
to even have more trouble uh controlling
themselves. But um you know I don't
think you should just leave a computer
there on the desk all day. That becomes
a distraction. Uh things like that. But
it's all about good use versus bad use.
But it's good use of technology, bad use
of technology and also good use of
humans in the room together and bad use
of humans in the room.
>> Yeah.
>> Thank you. Um we have just two minutes
left and um luckily I found out Amy that
you have a personal mission statement
just like S has a mission statement. So
I was hoping um we could close with
that. your personal mission is to show
what's possible in public education,
right? So, I'm wondering, can you share
with this audience one thing that you
want them to walk away believing is
possible?
>> Yeah. Um, you know, I think we're in
this space right now with um creativity
and innovation um with artificial
intelligence and what that can look like
to amplify the voice of our students. um
and that education now is accessible to
all kids. Um it's no longer based upon
your zip code. And so um I just
encourage everyone
um to not be afraid um to try new things
um and not be afraid of failure and um
that we're in this together. And I
firmly believe that to show what's
possible in public education. My
personal mission statement is that
because I I went to a private school and
I saw the relationship. I saw that I was
challenged at my level and unfortunately
when I dove into public I didn't see
that and um so just again wanting that
standard to be for everyone. So let's do
it. We're in this together.
>> Love it. Style of free worldclass
education for anyone anywhere. What
should we all believe is possible?
Well, I you know, I I I I sometimes
point out that if we were to go back 500
years before we had mass public
education,
depending where you go, global literacy
rates for men were like 20%, 25% and for
women were like 10 15%. And subjects
like algebra were considered esoteric
things that only like you know, super
experts would know. And I suspect if you
went to someone who could read and if
you pointed out some kid who's begging
on a corner in, you know, medieval
Europe and said, you know, should we
even try to teach that kid to read?
They're like, no, no, they're probably
not even capable of it. Um, now you fast
forward mass public education. You know,
it's easy to look at all the places we
need to improve education, but it's it's
sometimes good to just recognize how
much it's done for society, like in
terms of literacy rates and a
broad-based middle class, etc. We now
expect everyone to learn algebra.
Unfortunately, not everyone is, but you
know, we want them to, but we definitely
expect everyone to learn how to read,
etc. And and and where it's done well,
literacy rates are, you know, in the
high 90% area. Um, and but I like to
wonder, you know, what things do we
still have our blinders on, similar to
that person from medieval Europe, you
know, if I were to ask any of us,
including myself, what percentage of
kids could learn quantum physics or
learn how to make a machine learning
model or, you know, whatever, whatever,
I think most say, well, those are only
the top kids. But what if it's just
because, you know, so many kids
accumulate their gaps, don't have a
chance to, you know, they give up on
themselves essentially and everyone else
gives up on them. Uh, but if they're
able to really fill in those gaps, and
we get stories like this every day from
from learners all over the world. I
actually suspect a lot more people can
go much further than we think. And I
think it's a um it's a it's a must now
because we know the AIs are getting
really good. And I I don't think AI is
an excuse for all of us to collectively
cognitively cognitively offload. That's
that's a risky scenario. We have to
cognitively onload so that we can keep
up with the AIs. No one wants an editor
who can't write as well as the as the
staff writers which are now going to be

or a CTO who can't at least code as well
as as as the junior engineers which are

um I think yeah this this is the moment.

transcript.full_text: Hello everyone. Uh thank you so much for being here. Um I'm joined today by two people who I have a lot of respect for and I think um we're all going to get a lot out of this conversation. For those of you who have not met Dr. Amy Alzena, she is the definition of a trailblazer. She does a lot of things somehow also runs. Uh she's the superintendent of Cold Spring School District, one of the top academically performing districts in the state. She's uh she chairs the ACSA's artificial intelligent task force which um one of my colleagues sits on as well and uh so I'm very aware of the amazing work. Um and then she's super I'm sure I'm missing some things cuz there are a lot but Amy has been a real friend to other districts throughout California um supporting them in this like very exciting kind of turbulent moment as AI has hit education. S, I don't think you need an introduction, but I will just say that the the work that we're doing right now uh is is something I think everyone in this room is watching closely. So, thank you for being here, both of you. >> Thank you. >> Yeah, last week was a big one. Um you were both together at Q. Um S, Amy, you sat on a panel with Dr. Ruth Perez from LA County. Um there's a lot of honest conversation uh with people who are really running districts. So Amy, you were in both the panel and then in a smaller round table group with S. Has anything stuck out to you that you can share with us? >> Oh wow. Well, what resonated with me in that our discussion afterwards was when we were talking about in California the new math framework that came out and how everyone's just jumping at adopting a curriculum and I I even told myself, wait, am I missing something here or is this do we change the standards? What's happening? and and S you were saying something you know yeah it takes a couple years to understand a curriculum and I had just walked into um one of my teachers classrooms whose math charts math student achievement is off the charts and it was a third grade teacher yesterday and I was just doing a deeper dive on what is it that she's doing with her students and she said really it was last year when I had a new partner teacher I just hired her from my neighboring district and we did a deeper dive on the curriculum and we really identified some holes and how we needed to restructure the curriculum. Well, here we are fast forward with the same curriculum seven years later, right? And still learning that curriculum so we can get better at it. What works, what doesn't work, how we're utilizing Khan Academy as a supplement. And um I can only think in my head of like the what's going to happen right now as districts are just adopting these new crazy curriculum and what's going to happen to math achievement [laughter] and but s what you said really resonated with me how it it just takes a couple years to understand a new math curriculum and you're absolutely right. So I don't I you know that was my long winded way of saying wow um what's happening in the state right now. >> Yeah super appreciate that s did you hear that too curious if you saw it differently heard it differently than Amy did. No, definitely got that. And actually, it makes me think about, you know, we all spoke a lot and and I always try to speak a lot about uh on technology. You shouldn't just use technology for the sake of it. You should first take a step back and say like what are my goals here? Like what am I trying to what am I trying to solve for? And it I think in the context we're talking about it's usually we want to improve academic outcomes oftentimes is measured by state assessments, although there's a whole other thread about that too. Um, and the a and the the theory of action that we've had and I think Amy and others have had is hey if we can allow students to really fill in their gaps, learn at their own pace, personalize, get a lot of deliberate practice that that's going to improve outcomes and that's exactly what we try to do. So what you know whether it's using videos or software or AI or whatever we're trying to do on the technology side those are that that's truly the goal. If there's a way to do it with pencil and paper let's do it with pencil and paper. We don't need to use the fanciest technology. I think this uh curricular discussion that Amy's bringing up is actually the same thing. And you know, on the actual curricular decisions, I'm always a little bit of a of a bystander, but I've I we've Aviv, you know, we've paid close attention to it at Khan Academy for the last 15 or 20 years because we're always aligning to different standards, aligning to different curricula. And so I almost have a unique vantage point where I'm, you know, I'm doing the the the common core and Florida best and I'm still making videos for it. Just yesterday I made some, you know, illustrative math videos. I made some Florida best videos. So I see and and the reality is the heart of all of these curricula are the good parts are the same and and what you really want is um students to get more practice at their zone of proximal development. You want a teacher who feels comfortable um driving that holding the students accountable digging in deeper when appropriate using tools like Khan Academy where appropriate for personalization. And every time people are upset about test scores or they don't think things the reaction seems to be and it seems to be about every seven or eight years um let's get new curricula and there's you know the Gates Foundation ran a big study last year on high quality instructional materials and this isn't to you know throw shade on it but but they couldn't see an an effect um there um and and as Amy just mentioned every time you do that change management you kind of lose two three years like just just when you were um getting good at the old curric I I would argue that someone who is uh a teacher who's really strong at at a curriculum from 1980, especially in math, I mean, we could talk about other things, but that's going to be a good curriculum. And and someone who's struggling with the modern curricula, it's it's going to be harder. Another thing we did talk about, which I find fascinating, it's obviously a a bigger notion, is so much of um state so so much of of the perceived quality of schools, etc. are based on standardized tests. Um there's some merit to that, but maybe there's an opportunity to broaden the aperture. Uh right now, uh you know, there's things that we know are going to matter more and more in an AI world, like communication, collaboration, entrepreneurship. I mean, we could go down the list and those those types of things aren't captured uh right now, but maybe there are ways to capture it. So, that was the other interesting part of the conversation we had with some district leaders. >> Yeah. I'm curious. Actually, I'm remembering now that I wanted to ask you about this. Um Ruth Perez was was with you last week, right? >> Yes. >> Uh assistant superintendent of of LA County, which on the surface at least seems like it could not be more different than Cold Spring. Were you thinking about that at all, Amy? Like what do you know each other? Were you do do you know anything about how that district works? And >> yeah, great question. You know, I get that a lot because we're a single school district. We're small. I came from Santa Barbara Unified where I was 18 years, principal of two different schools and low soioeconomic, different demographics and um what I have learned through my 20 years as administrator is exactly what Sal said is when students are working within within their zone of proximal development, students are engaged and student achievement increases. And um I found that in my doctoral work why I had the best teachers ever and they were super [sighs] loving and kind but students were reading at a too high of a level or too low of a level so they weren't making growth and I was wondering what's going on. So I had to do a deeper dive. So it's for all areas right? We all have to be working in our sweet spot and it doesn't matter if you're in a large school district or a small school district. That's how we all learn. And so, um, going back to your question, um, no, you know, I I because we're talking about kids and all kids, um, learn the same and it's identifying what gets them excited in the morning, what is their purpose and how do we connect with them um, so that they want to come to school in the morning and not just the core subject areas, but our integrative approach to teaching and learning. And that's really the heartbeat of what we do um in education. >> Um I love that. Um and I I wish we had more time cuz I want to ask you about the transition from principal to superintendent. So if we have time, we'll come back to that. But um Sal, I wonder when you see success like this, right? Like academic achievement like we see in Cold Spring, what kind of questions arise for you uh around scalability? Yeah, I think that's that's the ultimate question. Um because we know that if um and we see this in multiple efficacy studies at Khan Academy, you know, if if students are engaged on the platform, they are seeing pretty dramatic growth. And people don't have to believe me, you know, go there's a lot of there's 50 plus, most of those are thirdparty studies um that we did not, you know, in any way fund or anything. Um and they they all show that if students are able to get even let's call it 30 to 60 minutes a week which isn't a lot um they're growing depending on the study 20 to 60% more than more than uh their their peers. And you know we have a few special cases of of places where they're more like 30 to 60 minutes a day and those type of places we're seeing growth of in some cases two to three grade levels a year. So, um, we know if if if the students are engaged there, it it's a and and and there's no magic there. It really is working on your zone of proximal development, getting a lot of and getting immediate feedback. Now, we know that from there are there's maybe five or 10% of students that if you just throw Khan Academy at them and say go, they get excited and they'll just be off to the races. But we know that most students aren't that. they need a really quality classroom environment um in in order to engage. And what we have learned there there's always been really from the beginning of Khan Academy uh tens if not hundreds of thousands of teachers who are also kind of this let's call it lean into Khan Academy saw a lot of that growth but it was in it was in pockets and so when we decided to do the district offering about eight years ago and we wanted to do more systemically with districts the big question is is um will a district especially if we're talking about large districts be able to get that type of consistency across of it and and and I I won't pretend like it's a completely solved problem. I think you have leaders like Amy where um she's, you know, she this is one of her top priorities. She believes in it. She's a force of nature. You know, everyone when Amy leaves the room, every like she's a force of nature. She is in the best in the best possible way. Um and and I think when you have investment at at the top like that, it does start to really pay those dividends. And we've seen that with Amy. We've seen that in large urban school districts uh like Newark, New Jersey. But it's when the the the superintendent, the chief academic officer, someone says, "Okay, you know, this is going to be my top priorities. I'm going to monitor the data. I'm going to provide soft accountability. Let's reward the the teachers who are engaged a lot and let's figure out how we can support the teachers um who need more help." >> Would you agree, Amy? >> I love that 100%. Um, and we see the same results. You know, whether again going back to doesn't matter what school you're at, the teacher that implements with Fidelity and really connects with kids, you're going to see a 2x growth. I mean, I I literally just met with a teacher, again, another teacher yesterday. Um, the average growth that we expect is between 40 to 60 um, student growth index. Her class average is 92. And this is our largest class of fourth grade students. We know the fourth grade students are challen it's a challenging sta group of standards but she does what's called an infield outfield model where she's working with a core group of students the other students are working independently at their level and they are so intrinsically motivated because also with it comes goal setting right it it's not just hey Khan Academy and go no let's set personal goals what are your goals are what is it that the actions that you're going to take to achieve those goals so you have that sense of purpose and then Lex support you so you can meet those goals and that's why those students outperform I mean every single year 100% of those students not only meet the standards but they score advance and that includes students with severe learning disabilities right because they're all working at their level um and so I I I couldn't agree more but it's not just hey just turn on the computer and I don't know why my kids aren't growing Well, because there's no interaction with the student, right? There's no relationship with the student. And I love what you S has in his book. I'm going to bring out this old one of yours, S. You have a chapter in here. Um it's it's called uh teaching as a team sport, right? And you talk in this chapter about coaching in here as teachers as coaches and and not as sage on the stage, right? And how important it is to be that coach and to have opportunities for student choice. And what I loved as I was interviewing my students and how they utilize Khan Academy, they love the choice. I'm going to work on unit two or I'm going to work on unit three. I love that there's choice and we love that as adults. Nobody likes to be told what to do, right? And they loved to progress monitor. Oh, here it is. I met this goal and now I'm going to move on to the next one. I mean, literally there was like almost like you could tell this intrinsic competition. I want to get to fifth grade because I know this, right? And that's excitement about learning and and that's not a model that's new. I mean, my education, we all take our, you know, what we learn from our own education. I literally had a model. It was called Accelerated Christian Education. It was It was ACE. It was all work pages. It was goal setting. You do five work pages a day. You had a goal chart, but you could get extra credit points and they were worth five cents for each page you did. And so I literally skipped two grades in math because I wanted to like I I want intrinsically I was like, I'm going to buy those roller skates for my sister out of the J C Penney catalog. I still remember I bought her Smurf roller skates. But it's that same type of concept of here's the goal and you're going to get rewarded if you go past that goal, right? And if I have a question I like it literally, this was this is going to be sound funny, but you put up your flag and and then the teacher went around and answered your question, right? But that worked well for me, but I also saw my friend where I like needed a lot more support from the teacher. So the teacher was with like my friend more, right? So it's very similar with technology. Now it makes it easier. Now I don't have a workbook. Now I can just do it adaptive learning. [laughter] >> That's too much, but yes. >> No, you're great. Um S, I wonder if it makes sense for you to speak a little bit now to we kind of rebuilt Khan Academy. And Amy, feel free to send immediate feedback. I you know the freshest feedback as [laughter] you roll out. There's a lot of uh changes we made. But in let's call it the classic Khan Academy, the one that's out there today. uh we have gotten the clear feedback that that is great but some students or maybe all could also benefit from some direction too like when in doubt what's the next thing that I should work on or the next two or three things so one thing that's in Khan Academy reimagined is it's a very clear learning path that's almost the most obvious visually thing you know the students will see their learning path now they can also continue to have the view that they've that they're maybe used to if they have been using Khan Academy where they can see all the units etc etc ETA. Um, another catalyst, frankly, was u federal accessibility guidelines. We were saying, "Okay, what would it take for us to redo all of our current?" We're like, "No, let's just rebuild it all and do it uh so it's all it's all accessible." For any of y'all who have been to San Jose, there's this old mansion that I think the the the woman who lived there 100 years ago was trying to confuse a ghost and um built a lot of stair staircases to nowhere or doors that just drop into a pit and things like that. But um I think any product that when you have years and years and years of incremental change and someone asks you, hey, add this, add that, we're like, oh yes, and you add it, but it doesn't it's not maybe not as easy to find. You have to hunt and peck and and click around a lot. So we really wanted to make it much clearer on how to use it, how to onboard it. Another thing, you know, there's so many use cases in education. Uh there's the teacher who might want to make the one-off assignment. There's the the teacher who want wants to do the more personalized learning. Uh students learning at their own time and pace. And historically, we part of the Winchester Mystery House is we were trying to meet all of these and we still are. But I think in this new product, you're going to see a stronger opinion um that we really do think the personalized mastery learning. Teachers can still make one-off assignments, but this one definitely has a bias towards teachers assigning the units for the year being very easy to monitor it. And we're already seeing really good data in the pilots that when teachers assign the units for the year, we're seeing depending on on the classroom, we're looking at 50 60% increase in skills leveled up. um on on Khan Academy. So loving that part. We're now it's it's it's a much more central character and and once again not just for the sake of throwing AI in there. it is to drive engagement to make sure kids are reflecting when they get something wrong, maybe get extra opportunities. Similarly, on the teacher side, it acts much more as a proactive teaching assistant. Uh, you know, internally we've called it, you know, KIGO concierge, Khan Academy concierge. So I think that's important in and students understanding that screen time can be addictive and and so for us at ColdSpring I get this question a lot especially because I'm you know AI task force chair I think parents so worried that our kids are on devices all the time which is not the case right and at all I mean we are so whole child um I said you know they're probably on a device max maybe in fourth through sixth grade an hour a day I would say um in the the lower grades more like 20 to 40 a minutes and I and I say our kids aren't getting addicted tech to technology at school I'm just going to be honest I tell you parents it's at home I mean we're utilizing it as a babysitter and I said I you know what I'm at fault right here's a phone you're at dinner you're out to dinner you need your little one to be quiet here watching watch this movie, right? Yeah, I always like once again take a step back and no don't make it like about technology versus not technology because I think even before the first computer entered a classroom you could visit two different classrooms and say oh this is one where this is really good for the kids and then another one where you're like hey I think the kids are kind of brain rotting even before technology existed and the difference the good one the one that you want is a lot of what we already talked about there's active learning the kids are engaged the teacher is engaged. And an and a not so great classroom is the kids are, you know, kind of just trying to sit in the chair, kind of passive, fingers on their lips. And so I think if we go into this world where now technology is another variable and 100% agree with everyone, it is it can be very addictive. So you have to be very very careful with it. But I think what we need to optimize for is but if we're talking about the classroom environments that are active learning either at your zone of proximal development and or learning other what do you would call it soft skills durable skills of communication intrapersonal skills uh it's being more personalized etc. So, it's not I if if a student is um using a screen to edit some really cool video they're making, if they're using a screen for digital art, if they're using a screen to write a paper, um if and and actually use it, do it themselves, if they're using a screen for research, if they're using a screen for Khan Academy, um I think those are are solid uses. And you know on average yeah you don't want it too much but I think any of us who've gone deep on something sometimes you get in focus mode and you might spend a couple of hour you know when I when I got really into programming when I was in high school yeah there were days where I would just get super focused and 12 hours I'm like where did that time go? So, and if we and if our frontal loes are apparently more developed, they're going to even have more trouble uh controlling themselves. And I firmly believe that to show what's possible in public education. My personal mission statement is that because I I went to a private school and I saw the relationship. We're in this together. >> Love it. Style of free worldclass education for anyone anywhere. What should we all believe is possible? Well, I you know, I I I I sometimes point out that if we were to go back 500 years before we had mass public education, depending where you go, global literacy rates for men were like 20%, 25% and for women were like 10 15%. And subjects like algebra were considered esoteric things that only like you know, super experts would know. Unfortunately, not everyone is, but you know, we want them to, but we definitely expect everyone to learn how to read, etc. And and and where it's done well, literacy rates are, you know, in the high 90% area. Um, and but I like to wonder, you know, what things do we still have our blinders on, similar to that person from medieval Europe, you know, if I were to ask any of us, including myself, what percentage of kids could learn quantum physics or learn how to make a machine learning model or, you know, whatever, whatever, I think most say, well, those are only the top kids. And I I don't think AI is an excuse for all of us to collectively cognitively cognitively offload. That's that's a risky scenario. We have to cognitively onload so that we can keep up with the AIs.
